author: mark
text:
Open question that ended the session: bl close DOES relate to remotes/code-shipping. I drafted close as balls-state-only (delete ball, push balls branch only) with code-ship outside balls — user said "you're super wrong about bl close being not related to remotes" and ended the session.

Right model TBD. Plausible readings:
- Close bundles code-shipping: the Phase C diff is a code change, not just a ball deletion. Today's review→close split may not survive.
- Close has dual arms (code-branch + balls-branch) shipped atomically through two commits in one op. The workflow verifier flagged dual-branch mechanics as unresolved.
- "Related to remotes" just means tracker push is integral (weak — I had said push happens).

Before next draft, ASK the user explicitly: does close ship code? If yes, spec the dual-branch mechanics (one worktree, two branches? two worktrees? one worktree with two commits?) concretely. Don't infer from current bl semantics.

author: mark
text:
GATE RESOLVED — bl-d66e settled the two-worktree question (closed 2026-06-01; see bl-2e26 "§ STATE TRANSITIONS" note). Consequences for this topic's live questions:

- The CODE worktree SURVIVES and is owned by a SIBLING delivery/worktree plugin (NOT core, NOT tracker). So close/drop do NOT themselves own its teardown — the delivery plugin's `close.post` / `drop.post` does. This topic's settled line "the worktree is core, so close/drop own its teardown" refers to the CHANGE worktree (`changes/<uuid>/`), which close/drop DO own via §8 phase E. Keep the two separate: phase E discards the ephemeral change worktree; the plugin's post-hook removes the long-lived code worktree.

- "drop from `open` (never-claimed) skips teardown?" — YES, for free. No claim ⇒ claim.post never ran ⇒ no code worktree exists ⇒ the delivery plugin's drop.post is a no-op under its idempotent create/remove contract. Not a special case — falls out of idempotency.

- Inviolable "no close from inside the worktree being torn down": under the resolved layout the torn-down worktree is the PLUGIN-owned code worktree (core runs close from operating/; the change worktree is ephemeral balls-state). So the rule becomes a delivery-plugin precondition (refuse if cwd is inside the code worktree), not a core check. Confirm where it lives.

- STILL OPEN — this topic's job — does close SHIP CODE? bl-d66e explicitly DEFERRED close.post here (the close-remote-coupling note below). But bl-d66e now CONSTRAINS the answer: `review.post` ALREADY does the squash-delivery (work/<id>→integration, writes delivered_in). So "does close re-ship code?" reframes to "what is left for close.post once review.post has already delivered?" Two readings:
  • close.post only tears down → the review→close split survives intact.
  • close must ship atomically (the user's "close IS related to remotes") → review.post's delivery moves INTO close.post and review becomes a bare state flip; the bl-d66e review sequence would need re-examination under that model.
  ASK the user (per the existing note), now framed concretely: given review.post does the squash, what does close add?

author: orionriver@gmail.com
text: RESOLVED. Answer: core close ships NOTHING — it just deletes the ball file (+ §5 close/drop trailer, + change-worktree teardown) with NO workflow enforcement. Code delivery already happened in the delivery plugin's review.post; the tracker plugin may push, but only the balls-state branches, never project code. The "does close ship project code / push the project remote" residue is a forge-process config question owned by bl-2a77 (delivery/worktree plugin, two variants), not a balls decision. Gate on bl-2a77 was backwards (close hands OFF to the plugin, doesn't depend on it) — removed, relinked relates_to. CLARIFIES the stale description line "the worktree is core, so close/drop own its teardown": that is the CHANGE worktree (changes/<uuid>/), not the code worktree. Full resolution recorded as § TERMINAL OPS note on bl-2e26; cwd-safety precondition carried forward to bl-2a77.
